analogy: Think of "Materials Metals and Non-Metals" through the chapter's own opening images: COMBUSTION AND FLAME COMBUSTION AND FLAME We use different kinds of fuel for various purposes at home, in industry and for running automobiles. Your list will contain fuels like cowdung, wood, coal, charcoal, petrol, diesel, compressed natural gas (CNG), etc. You are familiar with the... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: COMBUSTION AND FLAME COMBUSTION AND FLAME, 4.1 What is Combustion?, COMBUSTION AND FLAME 41, SCIENCE 42. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Materials Metals and Non-Metals is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cOMBUSTION AND FLAME COMBUSTION AND FLAME We use different kinds of fuel for various purposes at home, in industry and for running automobiles.

The text explains that your list will contain fuels like cowdung, wood, coal, charcoal, petrol, diesel, compressed natural gas (CNG), etc.

The text explains that you are familiar with the burning of a candle.

The text explains that what is the difference between the burning of a candle and the burning of a fuel like coal?

The text explains that may be you were able to guess right: candle burns with a flame whereas coal does not.

The text explains that similarly, you will find many other materials burning without a flame.

The text explains that let us study the chemical process of burning and the types of flame produced during this process.

The text explains that recall the activity of burning of magnesium ribbon perfor med in Class VII.

The text explains that we learnt that magnesium burns to form magnesium oxide and produces heat and light (Fig.

The text explains that we can perform a similar activity with a piece of charcoal.

The text explains that hold the piece with a pair of tongs and bring it near the flame of a candle or a Bunsen burner.

The text explains that we know that coal, too, burns in air producing carbon dioxide, heat and light.

The text explains that we were told that food is a fuel for our body.

The text explains that 4.1 : Burning of magnesium A chemical process in which a substance reacts with oxygen to give off heat is called combustion.

The text explains that the substance that undergoes combustion is said to be combustible.

The text explains that sometimes, light is also given off during combustion, either as a flame or as a glow.

The text explains that 4.2: Experiment to show that air is essential for burning Rightly so.

The text explains that can you infer anything at all about the role played by air in the process of burning?

The text explains that can you think of the reason why it stops burning?

The text explains that you will learn about this process in higher classes.

The text explains that cOMBUSTION AND FLAME 43 We find that a combustible substance cannot catch fire or burn as long as its temperature is lower than its ignition temperature.

The text explains that kerosene oil and wood do not catch fire on their own at room temperature.

The text explains that does it mean that ignition temperature of kerosene oil is lower than that of wood?

The text explains that the following activity shows that it is essential for a substance to reach ignition temperature to burn.

The text explains that do these experiences tell you that different substances catch fire at different temperatures?

The text explains that the lowest temperature at which a substance catches fire is called its ignition temperature.

The text explains that can you tell now why a matchstick does not catch fire on its own at room temperature?

The text explains that so, in the presence of water, the ignition temperature of paper is not reached.

The text explains that the substances which have very low ignition temperature and can easily catch fire with a flame are called inflammable substances.

The text explains that 4.6: Firemen extinguish the fire by throwing water under pressure Find out the telephone number of the fire service in your area.

The text explains that water cools the combustible material so that its temperature is brought below its ignition temperature.

The text explains that these are: fuel, air (to supply oxygen) and heat (to raise the temperature of the fuel beyond the ignition temperature).

The text explains that the job of a fire extinguisher is to cut off the supply of air, or to bring down the temperature of the fuel, or both.

The text explains that it can be stored at high pressure as a liquid in cylinders.

The text explains that so, it not only forms a blanket around the fire, it also brings down the temperature of the fuel.

The text explains that there are substances like phosphorus which burn in air at room temperature.

The text explains that explosion can also take place if pressure is applied on the cracker.

The text explains that cOMBUSTION AND FLAME 47 4.5 Structure of a Flame Activity 4.5 Light a candle (Caution : Be careful).

The text explains that recall that the sources of heat energy for domestic and industrial purposes are mainly wood, charcoal, petrol, kerosene etc.

The text explains that 3. hottest part moderately hot least hot wax candle outer zone of complete combustion (blue) middle zone of partial combustion (yellow) innermost zone of unburnt wax vapours (black) temperature?

The text explains that global warming is the rise in temperature of the atmosphere of the earth.

The text explains that sCIENCE 50 KEYWORDS KEYWORDS KEYWORDS KEYWORDS KEYWORDS ACID RAIN CALORIFIC VALUE COMBUSTION DEFORESTATION EXPLOSION FLAME FIRE EXTINGUISHER FUEL FUEL EFFICIENCY GLOBAL WARMING IDEAL FUEL IGNITION TEMPERATURE INFLAMMABLE SUBSTANCES WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT ‹ The substances which burn in air are called combustible. ‹ Oxygen (in air) is essential for combustion. ‹ During the process of combustion, heat and light are given out. ‹ Ignition temperature is the lowest temperature at which a combustible substance catches fire. ‹ Inflammable substances have very low ignition temperature. ‹ Fire can be controlled by removing one or more requirements essential for producing fire. ‹ Water is commonly used to control fires. ‹ Water cannot be used to control fires involving electrical equipment or oils. ‹ There are various types of combustions such as rapid combustion, spontaneous combustion, explosion, etc. ‹ There are three different zones of a flame - dark zone, luminous zone and non-luminous zone. ‹ An ideal fuel is cheap, readily available, readily combustible and easy to transport.

The text explains that explain how the use of CNG in automobiles has reduced pollution in our cities.

The text explains that can the process of rusting be called combustion?

The text explains that survey the availability of various fuels in your locality.

The text explains that find out their cost per kg and prepare a tabular chart showing how many kJ of various fuels you can get for every rupee.

The text explains that find out the number, type and location of fire extinguishers available in your school, nearby shops and factories.

The text explains that write a brief report about the preparedness of these establishments to fight fire.

The text explains that find the percentage of households using LPG, kerosene, wood and cattle dung as fuel.

The text explains that find out what precautions they take in using LPG.

The text explains that place a short candle and a slightly taller candle in a small dish filled with baking soda.

The text explains that place the dish at the bottom of a large bowl.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 4: Materials Metals and Non-Metals

prelims_traps: Do not treat COMBUSTION AND FLAME COMBUSTION AND FLAME and 4.1 What is Combustion? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Materials Metals and Non-Metals; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use COMBUSTION AND FLAME COMBUSTION AND FLAME and 4.1 What is Combustion? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
